draft.introduction: Genesis 3 recounts the fall: the serpent’s temptation, the eating of the forbidden fruit, and the expulsion from Eden. The serpent’s promise—“you will be like God”—frames the entire theosis question as a matter of means, not ends.

translation.text: And the serpent said to the woman, “You will not surely die. For God knows that on the day you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like gods, knowing good and evil.”
translation.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ ὄφις τῇ γυναικί · οὐ θανάτῳ ἀποθανεῖσθε · ᾔδει γὰρ ὁ θεὸς ὅτι ἐν ὧ ἂν ἡμέρᾳ φάγητε ἀπ αὐτοῦ διανοιχθήσονται ὑμῶν οἱ ὀφθαλμοί καὶ ἔσεσθε ὡς θεοί

translation.text: And God said, “Behold, Adam has become like one of us, knowing good and evil. And now, lest he stretch out his hand and take also from the tree of life and eat, and live forever—” So the Lord God sent him out of the garden of delight to work the ground from which he was taken.
translation.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ θεός · ἰδοὺ Αδαμ γέγονεν ὡς εἷς ἐξ ἡμῶν

translator_notes.note: The serpent’s promise “you will be like gods” (ἔσεσθε ὡς θεοί) is, paradoxically, true—God confirms it in 3:22. The problem is not the aspiration to godlikeness but the means: seizing it through disobedience rather than receiving it through participation. The Church Fathers argued that theosis is the legitimate fulfillment of what the serpent offered illegitimately.
